title: Sort and Reorder Lines
subtitle: :sort with flags handles unique, numeric, reverse, and key-based sorting.
summary: :sort sorts lines lexicographically. Flags switch on numeric, ignore-case, unique, and reverse modes. /pattern/ as a key sorts by the *post-pattern* portion of each line.

blocks.text: {key::}{key:s}{key:o}{key:r}{key:t} sorts a range of lines in place. With no range, the whole file. Combine flags to get the exact ordering you want without leaving the editor.

rows: Unique — drop duplicate lines after sorting
Sort by the part *after* leading whitespace

blocks.title: The /pattern/ key trick
blocks.text: By default {key::}{key:s}{key:o}{key:r}{key:t} compares whole lines. Add `/pattern/` and the comparator runs against the substring *after* the first match of `pattern` on each line — the pattern itself is skipped. So `:sort /,/` sorts by what comes after the first comma; `:sort /^\s*/` sorts ignoring leading whitespace. Add `r` after the slash and the comparator uses the *match itself* instead of what comes after.

blocks.title: Sort is in-place and stable
blocks.text: {key::}{key:s}{key:o}{key:r}{key:t} replaces the range's lines with their sorted permutation in a single buffer mutation, so it produces exactly one undo unit. The underlying algorithm is stable — equal-keyed lines preserve their input order — which matters when sorting by a `/pattern/` key that doesn't disambiguate every row.
To shuffle instead of sort, pipe through the shell: {key::}{key:%}{key:!}{key:s}{key:h}{key:u}{key:f} on Linux/macOS.
